body:
## Plan ready — BIN-79 Draft Milestone Planner

Implementation plan written and posted to the issue description.

**Summary:**
- 2 new files: `src/types/milestone.ts`, `src/lib/milestone.ts`
- 3 new components: `MilestonePlanner`, `MilestonePlanSetup`, `ChapterProgressList` with CSS modules
- 2 modified files: `page.tsx` (add milestone planner to both editor and corkboard layouts), `page.module.css`
- Forecast formula: compare actual words-per-day against required pace; "On track" / "At risk" badge
- Word count: derived from `loadContent(storyId)` — `content.trim().split(/\\s+/).filter(Boolean).length`
- Chapter progress: group scenes by `scene.chapter`, show planned/drafting/done counts
- Persists per story: `story-{storyId}-milestone-plan` localStorage key

Assign to: Code Monkey

## UX goal
Create a calm “finish engine” inside the story workspace that translates chapter progress into a believable path to draft completion, with low-friction controls writers can adjust in seconds.

## Information hierarchy
1. Milestone status summary (forecast state, target date, completion percent)
2. Next actionable milestone (what to write next and how much)
3. Chapter-level progress list (done/in-progress/remaining effort)
4. Plan controls (pause, edit, disable)
5. Historical velocity context (lightweight, confidence-oriented)

## Layout and components
- Place a new milestone planner card in the story dashboard/workspace context, not as a separate app area.
- Desktop: two-column section inside story workspace content area.
  Left column: milestone summary + next milestone CTA.
  Right column: chapter progress breakdown + lightweight velocity trend.
- Mobile: single column stack in priority order (summary -> next milestone -> chapter progress -> controls).
- Use card patterns consistent with existing surfaces (`border + subtle background + rounded corners`) and existing toolbar/header treatment.
- Add a compact “Plan settings” row with three tertiary actions: `Pause`, `Edit plan`, `Disable`.

## Interaction states
- Idle: show forecast pill (`On track` / `At risk`), target date, words remaining, next chapter milestone.
- Loading: use skeleton blocks matching card structure (summary lines, one forecast pill placeholder, 3-5 chapter rows).
- Empty: if plan not configured, show a focused setup prompt with 3 required fields (target length, target date, weekly cadence) and one primary action `Start draft plan`.
- Error: inline non-blocking error banner in-card with retry action; preserve last known numbers when available.
- Success: after setup/edit/pause/resume, show brief inline confirmation message near plan controls; do not use modal confirmation.

## Accessibility
- Keyboard: all controls reachable in logical order; milestone rows and actions operable via keyboard.
- Focus: keep visible 2px high-contrast focus rings aligned with existing `--color-carbon-600` focus treatment.
- Contrast: body text minimum against `--color-white`/`--color-polar-100` backgrounds; forecast badges must remain readable without color reliance (include icon/text state labels).
- Text clarity: use plain-language labels (`Words left`, `Weeks left`, `Next chapter target`) and avoid gamified/streak wording.

## Visual style
- Follow current grayscale token system from `src/styles/tokens.css`.
- Primary surfaces: `--color-white` and `--color-polar-100`; borders/dividers: `--color-fashion-300`.
- Primary text: `--color-black-700`; secondary/meta text: `--color-elephant-500` / `--color-cement-400`.
- Forecast emphasis should stay calm: use tonal badge treatments in grayscale (weight, border, icon) instead of saturated status colors.
- Preserve existing typography scale and spacing rhythm (`--font-size-*`, `--spacing-*`), with no new custom palette.

## Responsive behavior
- Desktop (`>=1024px`): milestone planner can sit beside existing writing-support content when available.
- Tablet (`768-1023px`): collapse to stacked cards with summary first.
- Mobile (`<768px`): prioritize one-tap actions and concise stats; avoid side-by-side metrics that force horizontal compression.
- Ensure controls remain one-click/tap for pause/edit/resume.

## Codebase alignment
- Shell/layout compatibility reviewed: `src/app/layout.tsx`, `src/components/AppShell/AppShell.tsx`, `src/components/ContentContainer/ContentContainer.tsx`.
- Story workspace anchor reviewed: `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` and `src/app/story/[id]/page.module.css`.
- Existing pattern references reviewed for reusable UI behavior: `Header`, `Navigation`, `ViewModeSwitch`, `Corkboard`, `SceneCard` components and CSS modules.
- Token constraints reviewed from `src/styles/tokens.css`; brief intentionally stays inside current grayscale primitives.

## Reuse vs new build
- Reuse:
  - Existing story-page structure and header region in `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx`.
  - Existing card, border, spacing, and focus patterns from current CSS modules.
  - Existing loading/empty language style from dashboard/stories/corkboard surfaces.
- New build:
  - Dedicated milestone planner UI block(s) in story workspace.
  - Forecast badge + next-milestone module.
  - Plan setup/edit/pause control grouping and chapter-progress rows tied to milestone data.

## Feasibility notes
- Current story page is client-rendered and already manages local state (`viewMode`, scenes); milestone UI can follow the same short-term local-state pattern before backend integration.
- Planner must not depend on corkboard availability; it should work in editor and corkboard modes with shared plan state.
- Forecast trust risk: include lightweight confidence framing (e.g., based on recent writing velocity) to avoid overpromising precision.
- Persist planner data per story, consistent with current per-story local persistence conventions.

## Constraints / implementation notes
- Primary insertion point: `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` within both editor and corkboard experience so users do not lose access by mode switch.
- Style additions should extend `src/app/story/[id]/page.module.css` and use existing tokens from `src/styles/tokens.css`.
- If structure grows, introduce a focused shared component under `src/components/` (e.g., milestone planner card) rather than bloating page-level JSX.
- Keep interactions non-modal and fast; setup flow should remain completable in under 2 minutes per acceptance criteria.

## Handoff
Assign to: Founding Engineer

## Decision
approve

## Differentiation thesis
This stands out if we position it as a "finish engine" (chapter trajectory + finish-date forecast + editable/pauseable plan) rather than a generic word-goal widget. The key differentiation is practical completion guidance inside drafting flow without punitive streak mechanics.

## Competitor evidence
- LivingWriter already offers word-count goals with due dates across chapter/manuscript/session scopes, so basic goal tracking alone is parity: https://guides.livingwriter.com/product-documentation/goals
- LivingWriter also emphasizes timers/sprints for productivity, which are useful but still largely session-based rather than manuscript trajectory forecasting: https://guides.livingwriter.com/product-documentation/timer-and-sprints
- Campfire positions strongly around modular worldbuilding/manuscript tooling (characters, maps, timeline, etc.), not a completion-forecast-first experience: https://campfirewriting.com/
- Inkitt emphasizes reader-feedback/community growth loops, which indicates a different value proposition than private draft completion planning: https://www.inkitt.com/writers

## Risks / trade-offs
- Forecast accuracy can erode trust if velocity swings widely; confidence signaling may be needed in design.
- Too much dashboard complexity could pull focus from writing flow.
- If we over-index on metrics, UX can drift toward stress/gamification (explicitly out of scope).

## Recommendation
Approve with one positioning guardrail: design the milestone planner as calm, adaptive guidance ("next best milestone" + editable assumptions) and avoid streak/punishment metaphors.

## Handoff
Assign to: Design

## Review Request
Requesting Market Research filtering on user value and retention impact assumptions.

- Focus: manuscript completion outcomes, not vanity streaks
- Ask: approve/reframe with competitor evidence for milestone + forecast UX

Assign to: Market Research
